title: The Macro Minute: What is the weak US dollar signaling for broader asset markets?

Happy Wednesday out there team 42. It's
your skipper here Darius Dale to present
our macro minute for Wednesday, May
21st, 2025. As always, we'll start with
the executive summary from today's
Ludlow Morning Note. If you would like
to review the analysis supporting these
conclusions and to see if you need to
make any changes in your portfolio to
remain on the right side of market risk,
you need to be a client of 42 macro. So,
today's key macro question is, what is
the weak US dollar signaling for broader
asset markets? Our answer,
the US dollar is declining today on
speculation the US might use the
upcoming G7 meeting in Banff at Bert
Alberta to advocate for a weaker
greenback among other factors. Traders
suspect President Trump and US Treasury
Secretary Scott Bessent back dollar
depreciation to support US manufacturing
and could leverage upcoming trade
negotiations to pursue it despite
official denials.
Japanese To that point, Japanese Finance
Minister uh Katsunobu Kato has already
confirmed he has scheduled bilateral
currency talks with Bessent during the
G7 summit. So, somebody's lying here. Uh
investors must be aware that over 40% of
S&P 500 revenue comes from abroad. So, a
weaker US dollar creates upside risk for
now depressed earnings projections. 2Q25
represents a very easy hurdle to beat,
especially for tech, materials, and
consumers communications services
sectors, which derive 56, 52, and 49% of
their revenue from abroad respectively.
Investors would do well to anticipate a
strong Q2 earning season this summer.
Consistent with our paradigm C
framework, we expect the total size of
the one big beautiful bill to increase
in the Senate where the demands for
populism would be much harder to ignore.
More debt-financed tax relief is
cyclically and structurally bullish for
US stocks and US corporate credit and
cyclically and structurally bearish for
the US dollar and US Treasuries.
If a strong Q2 earning season coincides
with the passage of the one likely
bigger beautiful bill, then both bears
and Trump 2.0 policy skeptics will be
forced into upside capitulation
throughout the second half of 2025,
fueling further gains in the 42 macro
kiss portfolio in the process. For what
it's worth, kiss bought Bitcoin back on
April 14th and the S&P 500 back on April
30th. Subscribe to receive our kiss and
Dr. Mo's signals in real time.
Transitioning to our 42 macro dashboard.
As always, I'll wrap up with a question
for our community. This one's regarding
the yen carry trade. It says with yield
surging, are we worried about another
unwind of the yen carry trade hitting
markets?
I'll start by saying I'm a professional
macro risk manager. I worry about
everything. I worry about the entire
distribution of probable economic
outcomes, whether it be right tail risk
or left tail risk. Consistent with how
we organize our our fundamental research
process, you know, we don't do what most
investors are doing, 90 plus percent of
investors are doing, which is I have a
thesis, therefore, and let me keep
banging the drum on this thesis and/or
continuing to do confirmation bias
oriented research to support that
thesis. We just do the same research on
the same topics all the time to identify
what that full distribution of probable
economic outcomes looks like. And yes,
the unwind
of yen carry trade unwinding
is a left tail risk in financial markets
as it relates to potentially blowing up
the global bond market and then
ultimately taking tanking risk assets
down with it. Now, when you think about
the integral of that distribution that
that left tail risk, the yen carry trade
left tail risk of that distribution, the
area under curve is not as high as the
area under the curve or some of the
right tail risk in that same full
distribution probable economic and
financial market outcomes. And so, it's
our job one, to identify what that
distribution is, two, identify the shape
of that distribution, three, identify
how the shape of the distribution is
changing as we learn new signals, new
things about the economy and new signals
from policy makers regarding their
intent from policy perspective. And with
that with respect to the yen carry trade
specifically, I would argue again, the
probability of a Yen carry trade blow up
it's much lower now than it was last
summer. Let me take you to my Bloomberg
here.
Got a chart showing the non-commercial
combined futures and options positions
of the speculative net position being in
the Japanese Yen. Obviously, we're using
this as a proxy for the aggregate size
of the Yen carry trade. And if you go
back to last July, this this low here in
terms of the Yen short position,
combined net short position in the
futures and options markets for the Yen,
which was at July 5th of last year
before the Nikkei peaked, before the S&P
500 peaked and had that sharp decline in
August. You know, the the the net short
position was at minus 190,000
contracts. Right now, we're at
plus 165,000
contracts. So, we got to completely
eviscerate the entire net short position
in the Japanese Yen in the currency
market. Now, again, this is not
the the the accurate size of the Yen
carry trade. There are legitimate
structural flows from a net
international investment positioning
standpoint as it relates to the US's $26
trillion net international investment
deficit that can potentially become
unwound if Japanese investors really had
a significant reason, and they may get a
significant reason if the negotiations
go poorly, by the way. If Japanese
investors had significant reason to
repatriate capital, then we will see an
unwind of the Yen carry trade. And I
would recall that the unwinding of the
Yen carry trade was very contributory to
the global financial crisis. Recall that
you had a lot of Japanese investors
piling money into more US mortgages,
US assets in general throughout the
early 2000s, and ultimately the unwind
of that starting in the second half, you
know, starting in the second half of
2020 2007, which really was contributory
to the decline that we saw in global
finance, the the the the sharp decline,
that's not sharp, the
the catastrophic and and permanently
destabilizing for some decline that we
saw in in the S&P and broader risk
assets during the global financial
crisis. So, yes, this is a risk.
You know, again, that full distribution
problem outcomes exist at all times.
Most investors when they're bullish are
only focused on the right tail. Most
investors when they're bearish only
focus on the left tail and the reality
is both tails always exist. It's really
our not our job to
only focus on one tail or another.
Rather, it's our job to identify what
the market is trying to price in in
terms of the tails, when it decides to
price in those tails, and ultimately
when it's done and decides to go from
pricing in one tail to the next tail.
That what I just described, figure out
what the market wants to price in. One,
understand what that distribution is
shaped like and how it's evolving over
time based on you know, a Bayesian
inference process full of data. Then
identify what the market is trying to
price in in terms of the tails and then
identify what the market no longer wants
to price in that particular tail and
identify specifically
and actionably when the market decides
to price in the next the other tail.
That's our research process in a
nutshell. That's an institutional grade
macro risk management process that
forces us and our clients to be, you
know, agnostic to what happens in a way
that allows us to constantly be making
money in financial markets. I don't care
what happens. What I care is if we're in
a bull market, I'm participating or if
we're in a bear market, I'm not
participating and I'm collecting I'm
clipping coupons and cash. That's my
goal. Your goal should not be to I want
to be right on the Japanese yen carry
trade unwinding so therefore I'm going
to be short. In so much that your goal
shouldn't be I want to be right on
Paradigm C therefore I'm going to be
long. No, that's not how to invest. Your
goal to invest is just to figure out
what Mr. Market is trying to price in
and participate. And if you don't want
to participate, the best thing you can
do in my opinion is do nothing as
opposed to fight the market. So,
hopefully
there's a lot of wisdom dropped in
there. Hopefully you guys appreciate
that and we will catch you back here
tomorrow. Cheers.
Mhm.
